title: Top Five Coming-of-Age Movies and ‘Are You There God? It’s Me, Margaret’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins review Kelly Fremon Craig's 'Are You There, God? It's Me, Margaret' and David Lowery's 'Peter Pan & Wendy,' then trade top-five coming-of-age lists before Sean sits down with Fremon Craig herself.

segments.heading: 'Are You There, God? It's Me, Margaret'

films.title: Are You There, God? It's Me, Margaret
films.note: Directed by Kelly Fremon Craig, adapting Judy Blume's 1970 novel. Both hosts cried. Amanda credits the movie with fully realizing the mother and grandmother, who barely register as characters in the book's first-person, kid's-eye telling; Rachel McAdams and Kathy Bates get real arcs of their own, including a plotline about the mother's suburban restlessness that Blume based on her own life.

films.title: Peter Pan & Wendy
films.note: David Lowery's live-action Disney adaptation. Both hosts pan it: flat performances, ugly digital sets, and a new Captain Hook backstory (raised as a Lost Boy, exiled by Peter) neither wanted. Sean says the few scenes shot on real locations are the only beautiful thing in the movie.

films.title: Peter Pan
films.note: The original animated film, referenced throughout. Amanda rewatched it and found the racial stereotyping much harder to sit with than she remembered, to the point she wouldn't show it to her son.

films.title: The Little Mermaid
films.note: New character posters just dropped at CinemaCon; Sean says the trailer hasn't looked strong.

films.title: Snow White
films.note: Directed by Marc Webb, who made '500 Days of Summer' and the 2010s Spider-Man movies.

films.title: Mufasa: The Lion King
films.note: Barry Jenkins directing; the hosts riff on which Shakespeare play it's secretly remaking (Richard III? Twelfth Night? Macbeth?) without landing on an answer.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: No. 1. Greta Gerwig, Amanda's hero. Set in 2002, a year younger than Amanda was at the time, down to the Dave Matthews needle drops.

films.title: The Graduate
films.note: Shown to her at a conservative high school. The floating-underwater, lost-at-sea imagery has stuck with her ever since.

films.title: The Virgin Suicides
films.note: Her Sofia Coppola pick, adapted from Jeffrey Eugenides: a group of sisters' story told through the neighborhood boys watching them from outside.

films.title: Reality Bites
films.note: Ben Stiller's Winona Ryder/Ethan Hawke/Ben Stiller love triangle. Amanda says it taught her what counted as cool in the '90s.

films.title: An Education
films.note: Carey Mulligan's breakout role, directed by Lone Scherfig: a high-achieving London teenager pulled into a more glamorous adult world before getting her life back on track.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: No. 1. Sean's favorite high school movie ever made; he's done a full 'Rewatchables' episode on it and interviewed Richard Linklater multiple times.

films.title: Boyz n the Hood
films.note: John Singleton's debut, made in his early 20s, with Laurence Fishburne as Furious Styles.

films.title: Water Lilies
films.note: Céline Sciamma's debut feature, which Sean discovered after 'Portrait of a Lady on Fire': a girl chasing a spot on her town's synchronized swim team.

films.title: Paper Moon
films.note: Peter Bogdanovich's black-and-white con-artist story, starring real-life father and daughter Ryan and Tatum O'Neal. Sean pairs it in his head with 'The Last Picture Show.'

films.title: Hoop Dreams
films.note: Steve James' three-hour documentary following William Gates and Arthur Agee toward basketball scholarships out of Chicago.

segments.heading: Honorable mentions
segments.note: the ones that didn't make the list

films.title: Ferris Bueller's Day Off
films.note: Sean's long-running argument: Cameron, not Ferris, is the one who actually changes in this movie.

films.title: The Breakfast Club
films.note: Shown in Amanda's high school too. Both hosts say it's less a personal coming-of-age story than portraiture of a whole social system.

films.title: Mean Girls
films.note: Amanda argues it belongs in a different bucket: more social satire than one character's personal reckoning.

films.title: Now and Then
films.note: Sean's example of the kind of funny ensemble movie missing from both their lists.

films.title: The Edge of Seventeen
films.note: Her directorial debut, also about a teenage girl. After it did well, she read a lot of scripts before landing on Margaret, the first thing that gave her the 'I need to write this' feeling.

films.title: Are You There, God? It's Me, Margaret
films.note: She hadn't reread the book since she was 11; this time she related to the mother and grandmother instead. Judy Blume was involved throughout, reading drafts and visiting set. Hans Zimmer scored it through his longtime relationship with producer James L. Brooks. Abby Ryder Fortson was cast as Margaret after Fremon Craig saw a long line of girls for the part.

films: Portrait of a Lady on Fire

Are You There, God? It's Me, Margaret.title: Are You There God? It's Me, Margaret.
